ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Mahi Dariya specifically. FOODON_03301116 (fish food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Indian spiced fish preparation. A00GJ (Fish and fish products) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 (Fish dish) is the closest available parent concept; no specific code for Mahi Dariya exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mahi Dariya is one marinated fish fillet of approximately 180 g, served with a lemon garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Fish and Fish Products (Rohu, Pomfret, and spiced fish preparations); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central — Fish, raw, mixed species (FDC ID 175168) for macro cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Mahi Dariya is a restaurant-specific preparation not found verbatim in ICMR-NIN or USDA. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN data for spiced/marinated fish preparations (rohu/pomfret category) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for raw fish macros, adjusted for buttermilk marinade and spice additions. The dish is primarily a fish fillet with minimal carbohydrate contribution from spices and buttermilk. Confidence is medium due to estimation from analogous preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fish and protein-dominant dishes; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mahi Dariya. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous protein-dominant fish dishes with spice marinades from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Fish-dominant dishes with minimal carbohydrate content consistently show GI in the 25–35 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 5.76) / 100 ≈ 1.7, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 3.2 × 1.8 = 5.76 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice marinade and buttermilk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful that Mahi Dariya contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 576 mg per serving), which contributes meaningfully to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mahi Dariya is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index (estimated GI 30) and high protein content (18.5 g per 100 g), which supports glycaemic control and satiety; the low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) results in a glycaemic load of approximately 1.7 per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with moderate unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mahi Dariya provides a favourable fat profile for cardiovascular health, with polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fats predominating over saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g), and the fish base contributes omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular risk reduction; sodium intake should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mahi Dariya is an Indian spiced fish fillet dish served at Indian restaurants in Singapore, notably at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry at Boat Quay. The dish consists of a fish fillet marinated in a blend of green chilli, clove, and cinnamon, then dipped in seasoned buttermilk before being grilled or pan-fried, and finished with a fresh lemon garnish. The name 'Mahi' derives from the Persian and Hindi word for fish (माही), while 'Dariya' means river or sea, evoking the dish's seafood character. The flavour profile is aromatic and mildly spiced, with the buttermilk marinade imparting a subtle tang and tenderness to the fish. The texture is firm yet moist, characteristic of well-marinated fish fillets. Nutritionally, Mahi Dariya is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 18.5 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-glycaemic diets. The estimated glycaemic index is 30 (low), and the glycaemic load per serving is approximately 1.7. The dish contains moderate sodium (~320 mg per 100 g) from the marinade, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension. The fat profile is predominantly unsaturated, supporting cardiovascular health. It is halal-suitable and gluten-free, though it contains dairy (buttermilk) and fish allergens. Mahi Dariya is typically served as a main course at dinner and pairs well with naan, rice, or Indian flatbreads.
common_user_queries: Is Mahi Dariya good for diabetes?
Mahi Dariya Indian House Boat Quay

confidence_reason: Mahi Dariya is a restaurant-specific dish not found verbatim in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from analogous ICMR-NIN spiced/marinated fish preparations and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central raw fish data. GI is estimated from analogous protein-dominant fish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the absence of an exact match in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source data for this food category. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as fish and buttermilk-based preparations do not contain industrially produced trans fats; this is naturally zero for this food type. 6. GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous protein-dominant fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 7. The dish name 'Mahi Dariya' is specific to the Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry restaurant at Boat Quay, Singapore, and may not be widely documented outside this establishment. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the fish and dairy ingredients and the Indian Muslim restaurant context; formal halal certification was not verified. 9. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are transliterations/translations of the dish name as no established native-language name exists for this restaurant-specific preparation.
